content:
# Mark Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 33v–34v (PDF pages 66–68) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_005_page_001.jpg`, `MRK_005_page_002.jpg`, `MRK_005_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ They came to the other side of the sea, to the region of the Gerasenes.

² When Jesus had stepped out of the boat, immediately a man with an unclean spirit met him from the tombs.

³ He lived among the tombs, and no one could bind him anymore, not even with a chain,

⁴ for he had often been bound with shackles and chains, but the chains were wrenched apart by him and the shackles shattered. No one had the strength to subdue him.

⁵ Night and day among the tombs and on the mountains, he was always crying out and cutting himself with stones.

⁶ When he saw Jesus from a distance, he ran and fell down before him.

⁷ Crying out with a loud voice, he said, "What have you to do with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I adjure you by God, do not torment me!"

⁸ For Jesus had said to him, "Come out of the man, you unclean spirit!"

⁹ And Jesus asked him, "What is your name?" He replied, "My name is Legion, for we are many."

¹⁰ And he begged him earnestly not to send them out of the region.

¹¹ Now a great herd of pigs was feeding on the hillside nearby.

¹² And the spirits begged him, "Send us into the pigs; let us enter them."

¹³ So he gave them permission. The unclean spirits came out and entered the pigs, and the herd, numbering about two thousand, rushed down the steep bank into the sea and drowned.

¹⁴ The herdsmen fled and reported it in the city and the countryside, and people came to see what had happened.

¹⁵ They came to Jesus and saw the man who had been possessed by the legion sitting there, clothed and in his right mind—and they were afraid.

¹⁶ Those who had seen it described what had happened to the demon-possessed man and about the pigs.

¹⁷ And they began to beg Jesus to leave their region.

¹⁸ As he was getting into the boat, the man who had been possessed begged to go with him.

¹⁹ But Jesus did not permit him. He said, "Go home to your own people and tell them how much the Lord has done for you and how he has had mercy on you."

²⁰ And the man went away and began to proclaim in the Decapolis how much Jesus had done for him, and everyone marveled.

²¹ When Jesus had crossed over again in the boat to the other side, a great crowd gathered around him by the sea.

²² Then one of the synagogue rulers, Jairus by name, came and, seeing him, fell at his feet

²³ and pleaded earnestly, "My little daughter is at the point of death. Come and lay your hands on her, so that she may be healed and live."

²⁴ And Jesus went with him. A great crowd followed and pressed in on him.

²⁵ Now a woman who had suffered from a flow of blood for twelve years,

²⁶ who had endured much under many physicians and spent all she had and was no better but rather grew worse,

²⁷ had heard about Jesus. She came up behind him in the crowd and touched his garment,

²⁸ for she said, "If I touch even his garments, I will be healed."

²⁹ And immediately the flow of her blood dried up, and she felt in her body that she was healed of her disease.

³⁰ At once Jesus, perceiving that power had gone out from him, turned in the crowd and said, "Who touched my garments?"

³¹ His disciples said to him, "You see the crowd pressing around you, and yet you ask, 'Who touched me?'"

³² But he looked around to see who had done it.

³³ The woman, knowing what had happened to her, came in fear and trembling and fell down before him and told him the whole truth.

³⁴ He said to her, "Daughter, your faith has made you well. Go in peace, and be healed of your disease."

³⁵ While he was still speaking, people came from the synagogue ruler's house saying, "Your daughter has died. Why trouble the teacher any further?"

³⁶ But overhearing what they said, Jesus said to the ruler of the synagogue, "Do not be afraid; only believe."

³⁷ He allowed no one to follow him except Peter, James, and John the brother of James.

³⁸ They came to the house of the synagogue ruler, and Jesus saw a commotion—people weeping and wailing loudly.

³⁹ He went in and said to them, "Why are you making a commotion and weeping? The child is not dead but sleeping."

⁴⁰ And they laughed at him. But he put them all outside, took the child's father and mother and his companions, and went in where the child lay.

⁴¹ Taking the child by the hand, he said to her, "*Talitha koum*," which means, "Little girl, I say to you, arise."

⁴² Immediately the girl stood up and began to walk—she was twelve years old. And they were overcome with amazement.

⁴³ He strictly charged them that no one should know this, and told them to give her something to eat.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Strong agreement across all three translators.** Mark 5's vivid narratives are read consistently from the manuscript.

1. **⚠️ "Gerasenes" vs. "Gadarenes" (v. 1):** Sinaiticus reads ΓΕΡΑΣΗΝΩΝ ("Gerasenes"). Vaticanus reads ΓΑΔΑΡΗΝΩΝ ("Gadarenes"). All three translators follow Sinaiticus. This is flagged because the two primary manuscripts disagree, though both point to the same general event.

2. **σώζω — "saved/healed/made well" (vv. 23, 28, 34):** EMET preserves the dual meaning with "be-saved." LOGOS renders "made well." PNEUMA renders "healed." I adopted LOGOS's "healed/made well" — it captures the primary sense while allowing the attentive reader to hear the deeper resonance. The double meaning of σώζω is one of Mark's recurring theological themes.

3. **"Overheard" vs. "ignored" (v. 36):** EMET notes the ambiguity of παρακούσας — it could mean "overheard" or "disregarded." All three translate as "overheard/overhearing." I agree this is the better reading in context.

4. **"Talitha koum" (v. 41):** All three translators preserve the Aramaic and agree this is a precious window into the historical tradition. The tenderness of the moment is universally recognized.

5. **The twelve-year parallel (vv. 25, 42):** All three translators note this narrative echo. PNEUMA's observation about Mark's "sandwich structure" illuminates the literary artistry.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### Verse 1 — Gerasenes vs. Gadarenes

- **Sinaiticus** reads ΓΕΡΑΣΗΝΩΝ ("Gerasenes") — followed by EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
- **Vaticanus** reads ΓΑΔΑΡΗΝΩΝ ("Gadarenes")

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows Sinaiticus with "Gerasenes." Both readings present geographical difficulties (Gerasa is ~30 miles from the lake; Gadara is ~5 miles). The variant likely arose from scribes attempting to improve the geography. Some scholars prefer the reading "Gergesenes" (found in other manuscripts), identifying a site directly on the lake shore.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
